ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers macadamia nuts as a commodity; baked/unsalted preparation facet not separately coded but the base commodity is an exact match.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of baked macadamia nuts is approximately 30 g (about 10–12 whole nuts), providing roughly 215 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Baked macadamia nut, unsalted — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database (F09030007)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F09030007 for baked macadamia nut, unsalted. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12131 (Nuts, macadamia nuts, dry roasted, without salt added) where HPB does not report those fields, as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Nuts — Analogous estimate based on published GI values for mixed nuts and macadamia nuts; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for baked macadamia nuts, unsalted, exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 10 (low) based on: (1) macadamia nuts are predominantly fat (76 g/100g) and fibre (8 g/100g) with only 13.4 g/100g carbohydrate, of which a significant portion is fibre; (2) published GI values for mixed nuts range from 15–25 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); (3) macadamia nuts have lower available carbohydrate than mixed nuts, supporting a GI estimate at the lower end of the nut range. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (10 × 1.61 g net carb per 30g) / 100 ≈ 0.16, rounded to 0.4 using total carbohydrate basis (13.38 × 0.30 × 10 / 100 = 0.40).

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (4 mg/100g) and high MUFA content supporting vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Baked unsalted macadamia nuts are appropriate for hypertension management due to negligible sodium (4 mg/100g) and a dominant monounsaturated fat profile (oleic acid ~58.9 g/100g) that supports endothelial function; limit to 30 g per serving to manage caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~10), high dietary fibre (8 g/100g), and low available carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baked unsalted macadamia nuts have a very low estimated GI (~10) and provide 8 g dietary fibre per 100g, resulting in minimal postprandial glucose impact; a 30 g portion is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes, provided total caloric intake is monitored given the high energy density of 718 kcal/100g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dominant MUFA (palmitoleic and oleic acid ~58.9 g/100g), zero trans fat, and high dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Macadamia nuts have one of the highest MUFA contents of any nut (predominantly oleic and palmitoleic acid), zero trans fat, and 8 g/100g dietary fibre, supporting LDL reduction and cardiovascular risk management; saturated fat at 12.1 g/100g warrants portion control at ~30 g per day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at upper boundary of MUFA/PUFA carve-out threshold (~12.1 g/100g); low omega-3 content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Macadamia nuts may be included cautiously in a rheumatoid arthritis diet given their dominant MUFA fraction and anti-inflammatory fibre content, but their saturated fat (~12.1 g/100g) is at the upper limit of the clinical carve-out threshold and their omega-3 content is low; complement with walnuts or flaxseed for optimal omega-3 balance and limit to 30 g per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked macadamia nuts (unsalted) are whole macadamia nuts (Macadamia integrifolia or M. tetraphylla) that have been dry-roasted or baked without the addition of salt or oil. Originating from Australia, macadamia nuts are prized for their rich, buttery flavour and smooth, creamy texture. They are among the highest-fat nuts available, with 76 g of fat per 100g, of which approximately 77% is monounsaturated fat (predominantly oleic and palmitoleic acid), making them nutritionally comparable to olive oil in fatty acid profile. Each 100g provides 718 kcal, 7.79 g protein, 13.38 g carbohydrate, 8 g dietary fibre, and only 4 mg sodium. The very low sodium content and dominant MUFA profile make unsalted baked macadamia nuts suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Their estimated glycemic index of approximately 10 (low) and high fibre content make them appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed in controlled 30 g portions. Macadamia nuts are a good source of thiamine (0.71 mg/100g), magnesium (130 mg/100g), and manganese. They are naturally cholesterol-free, gluten-free, vegan, and halal. Common uses include snacking, trail mixes, baking, and as a topping for salads or desserts. The baked preparation concentrates flavour without adding sodium or significant additional fat compared to raw nuts.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F09030007 (confidence 0.95 per input). Fatty acid breakdown, micronutrients, and water content supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12131 (dry roasted macadamia nuts, without salt added), which is an exact preparation match. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo F09030007. 2. Saturated fat (12.06 g), monounsaturated fat (58.87 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.50 g), trans fat (0.0 g), potassium (368 mg), cholesterol (0 mg), vitamin C (0.7 mg), vitamin A (0 mcg RAE), thiamine (0.71 mg), riboflavin (0.16 mg), niacin (2.47 mg), vitamin B6 (0.28 mg), vitamin B12 (0 mcg), folate (11 mcg DFE), vitamin D (0 mcg), vitamin E (0.54 mg), phosphorus (188 mg), magnesium (130 mg), zinc (1.30 mg), selenium (3.6 mcg), and water (1.36 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12131 (Nuts, macadamia nuts, dry roasted, without salt added) as HPB does not report these fields. 3. No zero-macro justification required: protein_g (7.79), fat_g (76.08), and carbohydrate_g (13.38) are all non-zero. 4. trans_fat_g set to 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB 12131; macadamia nuts contain no industrially produced trans fats. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; macadamia nuts are a plant food and contain no vitamin B12. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central. 7. vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; plant-based nut with no vitamin D. 8. GI estimated at ~10 (low) based on analogous nut GI values from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for baked macadamia nuts exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 9. dietary_tags 'wholegrain' removed from output as macadamia nuts are not a grain product; retained plant_based, low_sodium, high_fibre, high_fat_warning. 10. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC used as defensible matches for macadamia nut commodity; SNOMED CT 227493005 is a substance-level match. Baked/unsalted preparation facet not separately coded in FoodEx2 but base commodity is exact.
